prd_body:
Target: C6

## Problem statement
Kigo is free, but its one paid feature — putting today's image+Kigo on the home-screen **Widget** — is unlocked by a single auto-renewable "widget access" subscription (the **Entitlement**). Today the app has no StoreKit layer at all: no product, no entitlement state, no shared store. Until an Entitlement can be purchased, persisted, read back, and restored, the **Widget Gate** (C7) has nothing to gate on, and a subscriber has no way to pay for the thing the subscription reveals.

The C6 evidence procedure (`-only-testing:KigoTests/EntitlementTests`) currently fails because the suite does not exist (0 tests). There is no `Kigo.storekit` configuration, the `StoreKitTest` framework is not linked to the test target, and no entitlement provider or injectable shared store has been built.

## Solution
The app ships a single auto-renewable "widget access" subscription. From the user's perspective: with no purchase they are not entitled (the widget will show the Kigo name without the image); after purchasing, the Entitlement becomes active and stays active across launches; and on a fresh install / new device, **Restore** re-establishes the active Entitlement from their App Store history. The active state is written somewhere the **Widget** can later read it, so the home-screen experience can react without re-running purchase logic.

All of this is verifiable in-process: the Entitlement is derived from StoreKit transactions and exercised with the `StoreKitTest` framework against a committed `Kigo.storekit` configuration — no App Store Connect dependency, no live network (per Constraints).

## User stories
1. As a non-subscriber, when no transactions exist, the entitlement provider reports the Entitlement **inactive**, so the Widget Gate later hides the image.
2. As a user, when I purchase the `com.tomeitotameigo.kigo.widgets.monthly` product, the entitlement provider reports the Entitlement **active**.
3. As a user, after I purchase, the active state is **written to the shared store** (the injectable seam the widget reads), so the entitlement need not be recomputed by the widget process.
4. As a returning user on a new device, when in-memory state is cleared and transactions are synced (**Restore**), the entitlement provider reports the Entitlement **active** again.
5. As a user, a single **Paywall** screen offers the "widget access" subscription with a Restore action. (Product surface; only the entitlement engine below it is gated by the C6 evidence procedure — see Testing decisions.)

## Implementation decisions
- **`Kigo.storekit` configuration** (committed): defines one auto-renewable subscription product, id `com.tomeitotameigo.kigo.widgets.monthly`, in a single subscription group ("widget access"). This is the StoreKitTest fixture; no App Store Connect.
- **Entitlement provider** — a deep module that derives Entitlement state from StoreKit. It reads current entitlements / verified transactions and exposes a simple `isActive`-style read plus a way to refresh/sync (for Restore). It owns the StoreKit interaction so the rest of the app depends only on a small Entitlement-state surface.
- **Injectable shared store** — a small protocol abstracting the app↔widget shared store (the "active" flag the widget reads). Production implementation backed by app-group `UserDefaults` (app group `group.com.tomeitotameigo.kigo` per Constraints; the loop may refine the suite name). Tests inject an in-memory fake so the write is observable in-process without provisioning. This is the seam C7's widget will read from.
- **Paywall** view — a minimal SwiftUI screen presenting the product and a Restore button, wired to the entitlement provider. Kept thin; its rendering is not part of the C6 evidence procedure.
- **Project/scheme wiring** — link the `StoreKitTest` framework to the `KigoTests` target and attach the committed `Kigo.storekit` configuration to the Kigo scheme's test action, so `xcodebuild test` runs against it. Update `project.yml` (XcodeGen) accordingly; the `.xcodeproj` stays gitignored (ADR 0003).
- A new ADR should record the entitlement-provider seam and the shared-store abstraction (written during implementation, following the ADR 0006–0008 convention).

## Testing decisions
The criterion's evidence procedure is the north star: `EntitlementTests` in `KigoTests`, using `StoreKitTest` against the committed `Kigo.storekit`, asserting external behavior only:
- with no transactions, the provider reports **inactive**;
- after buying `com.tomeitotameigo.kigo.widgets.monthly`, it reports **active** and writes the active flag to the **injected** shared store (assert the fake store observed the write);
- after clearing in-memory state and syncing transactions (Restore), it reports **active** again.
Drive purchases through a `SKTestSession` (StoreKitTest) — never real StoreKit. Inject the shared store (in-memory fake) and any date/dependency seams rather than touching app-group `UserDefaults` from tests. The suite must contain ≥1 test (guards against vacuous success). Prior art: the `ContentSource`/`ContentStore` seam tests (`ContentSourceTests`) show the injectable-dependency style used here.

## Judgment claims in scope
- **J1** (whole project — calm, tasteful wellness object): the Paywall screen is part of the app's feel, so a Paywall view slice should route to the judge panel. No J* covers the entitlement engine itself; J2 (C2/C5) does not apply here.

## Out of scope
- The **Widget Gate** itself (C7) — this milestone only produces the Entitlement + shared store the widget will read.
- Browsing other days, user accounts/sync, notifications, sharing/onboarding, a content backend, real photography/art, pre-iOS-26 support (GOAL.md fence).
- Server-side receipt validation, multiple products / pricing tiers / promo offers, subscription management UI beyond a single Restore action, and any App Store Connect configuration — none are required to flip C6.

## Decomposition note (operator amendment, 2026-06-13)

<!-- afk-operator-amendment: decomposition-shape -->
The entitlement provider is **expected to be built by growing one type across several
thin vertical slices** — `inactive → active-on-purchase → persists-to-shared-store →
restore` — where each slice ships exactly one new behavior **together with the tests
that prove it**. This incremental growth of `EntitlementProvider`'s API across
consecutive slices is the **intended decomposition shape**, NOT a horizontal/layer
split. A later slice that extends or modifies the provider an earlier slice delivered
is expected and correct.

In particular: **do not require purchase-activation and the shared-store write to ship
as one atomic slice.** "Purchase makes the entitlement active" and "activation writes
the active flag to the injected shared store" are two distinct, independently-verifiable
behaviors, each carrying its own test — they may (and should) be separate thin slices.
A slice is a forbidden horizontal split ONLY if its deliverable is tests-only, or
production-code-only whose tests live in another slice — never merely because it touches
code an earlier slice already merged.


## Slices
- [x] #38 StoreKitTest harness reports the Entitlement inactive when no transactions exist
- [ ] #39 Purchasing the widget-access product makes the Entitlement active
- [ ] #40 Activation writes the active flag to an injected shared store
- [ ] #41 Restore re-establishes the active Entitlement after in-memory state is cleared
- [ ] #42 Paywall screen offers the widget-access subscription with a Restore action
rejection_history:
**afk-slice-blocked**: critic rejected 3 decompositions — could not converge within the ≤2 re-decomposition budget. Nothing was published.

**Standing violation (final round):** the adversarial critic treats *purchase → active* and *active write reaches the injected shared store* as a single atomic behavior that must ship in one slice, and flags any slice that incrementally extends `EntitlementProvider`'s API (e.g. adding the shared-store injection in a later slice) as a horizontal/layer split because it "retroactively modifies production code already delivered."

**Why this needs a human:** that standard is in direct tension with the "prefer many thin slices; when in doubt, split" rule. Every thin vertical slice that grows an existing type's surface (the natural shape of a TDD-built provider: inactive → active-on-purchase → write-to-store → restore) is read by the critic as a forbidden split. The decomposer cannot satisfy both "split thin" and "never extend an already-merged type" simultaneously for this PRD.

**Decomposition rejection history:**
- Round 1 — ACs stated implementation/artifacts (framework-linked, hard-coded-flag constraint, "protocol exists", "ADR recorded") rather than machine-checkable behavior; Paywall slice had no test-verifiable AC.
- Round 2 — those ACs fixed, but Slice 0 became a tests-only harness (no production behavior); a metadata-only AC remained; a Slice-3 AC described suite contents.
- Round 3 — Slice 0 made a true vertical walking skeleton (provider reports inactive) and the non-behavioral ACs removed, but the critic then rejected splitting purchase-activation (Slice 1) from the shared-store write (Slice 2) as a horizontal behavior split.

**Suggested upstream resolutions (for the human):**
- Merge purchase-activation + shared-store write into one slice (accept thicker slices here), OR
- Refine GOAL.md / this PRD to state that incrementally growing the entitlement provider across thin slices is the intended decomposition shape, so the critic stops treating API extension as a layer split.

The integration branch `integration/37-widget-entitlement` was created during SLICE preflight and left intact (harmless; the next SLICE run will reuse it).
